perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'in compliance with all applicable Laws including, without limitation, cGMPs' from Section 5.2(e)(ii) weakens the guarantee that products meet legal standards. Omitting 'within the meaning of Sections 501 and 502, respectively, of the Act or any other applicable Law;' from Section 5.2(e)(iv) also weakens compliance with the FD&C Act. Deleting 'as specified in the applicable Facility Addendum' from Section 5.2(e)(v) loosens the commitment to shelf life compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 351 defines adulteration for drugs and devices. By removing the explicit requirement to comply with 'all applicable Laws including, without limitation, cGMPs' and the reference to specific sections of the FD&C Act related to adulteration and misbranding, the modified contract now contradicts the FD&C Act, as it does not guarantee that products will meet federal safety standards, potentially misleading the customer about their rights.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandated compliance with 'all applicable Laws including, without limitation, cGMPs' and explicitly referenced sections 501 and 502 of the Act regarding adulteration and misbranding. The modified text removes these explicit legal compliance requirements, directly contradicting the FD&C Act by not ensuring products meet federal safety standards. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation support the conclusion that deleting these clauses weakens the guarantee of legal compliance, creating a significant contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from GovInfo, further implies that without these guarantees, the contract could be interpreted as not adhering to federal safety standards.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the terms and conditions set forth herein, Customer may conduct, at its own expense, periodic quality audits, to ensure Manufacturer's compliance with the terms of this Agreement. Manufacturer shall cooperate with Customer's representatives for all of these purposes, and shall promptly correct any deficiencies noted during the audits. Any Records or information accessed or otherwise obtained by Customer or its representatives during any such inspection or audit or any visit at any Facility shall be deemed Manufacturer's confidential and proprietary Information and each representative of Customer will be subject to non-use and other confidentiality obligations substantially comparable to those set forth herein for Customer.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer may conduct, at its own expense, periodic quality audits. Manufacturer shall cooperate with Customer's representatives for all of these purposes. Any Records or information accessed or otherwise obtained by Customer or its representatives during any such inspection or audit or any visit at any Facility shall be deemed Manufacturer's confidential and proprietary Information and each representative of Customer will be subject to non-use and other confidentiality obligations substantially comparable to those set forth herein for Customer.
perturbation.explanation: The section 'to ensure Manufacturer's compliance with the terms of this Agreement. Manufacturer shall promptly correct any deficiencies noted during the audits' has been omitted. This weakens customer's ability to ensure product quality and safety, potentially contradicting cGMP (Current Good Manufacturing Practice) requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.180 requires written procedures for production and process controls designed to assure that the drug products have the identity, strength, quality, and purity they purport or are represented to possess. By removing the manufacturer's explicit obligation to promptly correct deficiencies noted during the audits, the modified text weakens the guarantee of maintaining cGMP standards as required by law.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'Manufacturer shall promptly correct any deficiencies noted during the audits' significantly weakens the manufacturer's obligation to adhere to quality standards. The Law Explanation states 21 CFR § 211.180 requires written procedures for production and process controls to ensure drug products maintain their identity, strength, quality, and purity. By removing the explicit obligation to correct deficiencies, the changed text creates a contradiction by weakening the guarantee of maintaining cGMP standards. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that this content is from the eCFR and is authoritative. Therefore, the change directly undermines the requirement for maintaining quality and purity.

perturbation.original_text: Manufacturer shall order and maintain sufficient quantities of all Product Materials, including safety stock as required by the applicable Facility Addendum, to enable Manufacturer to manufacture and Deliver Product in accordance with its Delivery obligations under this Agreement and the applicable Facility Addendum. With respect to those Third Party suppliers of Product Materials used by Manufacturer or its Affiliates in the ordinary course in the manufacturing or supply of a given Product immediately prior to the Effective Date ("Manufacturer Third Party Suppliers"), Manufacturer shall be permitted to purchase solely the same Product Materials from such Manufacturer Third Party Suppliers in connection with its activities under this Agreement without first obtaining Customer's prior written consent. Any other Third-Party supplier for Product Materials (or procurement of a different Product Material from any Third-Party supplier) must be approved in advance in writing by Customer (such approval not to be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed). For the avoidance of doubt, as of the Effective Date, as between Manufacturer and Customer, Manufacturer will be solely responsible for maintaining and establishing relationships with the Third-Party suppliers of Product Materials. The costs of all such Product Materials shall be included in the Price of the applicable Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Manufacturer shall order and maintain sufficient quantities of all Product Materials to enable Manufacturer to manufacture and Deliver Product in accordance with its Delivery obligations under this Agreement and the applicable Facility Addendum. With respect to those Third Party suppliers of Product Materials used by Manufacturer or its Affiliates in the ordinary course in the manufacturing or supply of a given Product immediately prior to the Effective Date ("Manufacturer Third Party Suppliers"), Manufacturer shall be permitted to purchase solely the same Product Materials from such Manufacturer Third Party Suppliers in connection with its activities under this Agreement without first obtaining Customer's prior written consent. Any other Third-Party supplier for Product Materials (or procurement of a different Product Material from any Third-Party supplier) must be approved in advance in writing by Customer (such approval not to be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed). For the avoidance of doubt, as of the Effective Date, as between Manufacturer and Customer, Manufacturer will be solely responsible for maintaining and establishing relationships with the Third-Party suppliers of Product Materials. The costs of all such Product Materials shall be included in the Price of the applicable Product.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'including safety stock as required by the applicable Facility Addendum' has been omitted. This creates a legal risk by potentially violating cGMP requirements that mandate adequate safety stocks of materials to prevent disruptions in manufacturing.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.84 outlines requirements for testing and approval of components and drug product containers and closures. While not directly mandating 'safety stock,' the regulation necessitates controls to ensure materials meet standards. By removing the requirement for 'safety stock as required by the applicable Facility Addendum,' the manufacturer has more flexibility to not adequately account for material variability that could cause potential compliance issues related to FDA regulations.

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'including safety stock as required by the applicable Facility Addendum' creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating cGMP requirements. While the law snippets do not explicitly mention 'safety stock,' the Law Explanation clarifies that 21 CFR § 211.84 necessitates controls to ensure materials meet standards. The modified text, by removing the safety stock requirement, increases the risk of material variability issues that could cause potential compliance issues related to FDA regulations as the manufacturer now has more flexibility not to account for variability. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition: 'Omissions occur when a contract removes essential information, creating legal loopholes. A legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant.'
